summary: CSUR acceptance pass: prior-art scan (web + ACM DL), differentiation, framework-originality, takeaways, completeness gaps

papers.title: Fairness in Large Language Models: A Taxonomic Survey
papers.authors: Zhibo Chu, Zichong Wang, Wenbin Zhang
papers.venue: ACM SIGKDD Explorations Newsletter (Vol. 25, Issue 2)
papers.overlap: Direct parent lineage — taxonomizes fairness in LLMs that answer (intrinsic/extrinsic bias, debiasing generation). Senior author Wenbin Zhang is a co-author on our draft. Provides our definitional grounding and venue anchor.
papers.how_ours_differs: Ours is the explicit agentic successor: Chu et al. cover single-turn QA and static generation only; they do not treat tool selection, memory/RAG, multi-agent delegation, planning, user modeling, or long-horizon drift. Our survey takes the agentic pipeline as its organizing axis, which Chu et al. explicitly do not address.

papers.title: Evaluation and Benchmarking of LLM Agents: A Survey
papers.authors: Mahmoud Mohammadi, Yipeng Li, Jane Lo, Wendy Yip
papers.venue: arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.21504
papers.overlap: Surveys evaluation methodology for LLM agents broadly; fairness appears as a brief sub-subsection (3.4.1) inside the Safety and Alignment chapter, estimated at a few paragraphs of a 40+ page document. Uses a two-dimensional taxonomy (evaluation objectives × evaluation process). Explicitly cited in our draft as a near-neighbor (mohammadi2025evaluation).
papers.how_ours_differs: Fairness is one tiny sub-topic among many evaluation objectives; our survey takes fairness as the *sole* organizing axis. The paper does not organize by agent component, does not propose action-level disparity metrics, and does not synthesize bias mechanisms per component.

papers.title: Agentic AI in Healthcare & Medicine: A Seven-Dimensional Taxonomy for Empirical Evaluation of LLM-based Agents
papers.authors: Shubham Vatsal, Harsh Dubey, Aditi Singh
papers.venue: arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.04813
papers.overlap: Proposes a seven-dimensional taxonomy for evaluating LLM agents in healthcare; fairness is one of seven dimensions (not the organizing axis) and the treatment is largely conceptual rather than empirically grounded. Explicitly cited in our draft as a near-neighbor (vatsal2026agentic).
papers.how_ours_differs: Single domain (healthcare), fairness is one of seven dimensions, no component-organized bias analysis, no action-level metrics, no cross-domain or multi-component synthesis. Ours is fairness-dedicated, cross-domain, and tied to executable CFR/MASD/action-disparity instruments.

papers.title: Counterfactual Fairness Evaluation of LLM-Based Contact Center Agent Quality Assurance System
papers.authors: Kawin Mayilvaghanan, Siddhant Gupta, Ayush Kumar
papers.venue: arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.14970
papers.overlap: Applies CFR and MASD metrics — the exact metrics we generalize — to a single-domain (contact center QA) LLM agent. Introduces the 13-dimension taxonomy (Identity, Context, Behavioral Style) for that one use case. Explicitly cited in our draft as mayilvaghanan2026counterfactual.
papers.how_ours_differs: Restricted to one domain and one agent role (QA evaluator in contact centers). No component decomposition across tool selection, memory, multi-agent, planning, user modeling, or long-horizon. Our survey generalizes CFR/MASD across all six agent components and multiple consequential domains — this is a specific single-domain application paper, not a survey.

papers.title: A Survey on Trustworthy LLM Agents: Threats and Countermeasures
papers.authors: Miao Yu, Fanci Meng, Xinyun Zhou, Shilong Wang, Junyuan Mao, Linsey Pang, Tianlong Chen, Kun Wang, Xinfeng Li, Yongfeng Zhang, Bo An, Qingsong Wen
papers.venue: arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.09648
papers.overlap: Surveys trustworthiness of LLM agents including safety, privacy, truthfulness, and fairness across intrinsic components (brain, memory, tool) and extrinsic aspects (user, agent, environment). Overlaps structurally with our component anatomy.
papers.how_ours_differs: Fairness is one of five or more trustworthiness dimensions alongside safety, privacy, robustness, etc. The organizing axis is threats-and-countermeasures, not fairness mechanisms by component. Our survey is fairness-only, provides a dedicated evaluation framework (CFR/MASD/action-level disparity), a mitigation map tied to components, and an empirical bias audit — none of which appear in this paper.

papers.title: Fairness in Agentic AI: A Unified Framework for Ethical and Equitable Multi-Agent System
papers.authors: Rajesh Ranjan, Shailja Gupta, Surya Narayan Singh
papers.venue: arXiv preprint arXiv:2502.07254
papers.overlap: Self-described as 'a comprehensive survey of fairness in multi-agent AI' with a unified framework treating fairness as an emergent property of agent interactions. Addresses emergent biases, systemic inefficiencies, conflicting incentives, fairness constraints, and bias mitigation strategies in multi-agent systems.
papers.how_ours_differs: This is only 12 pages (4 figures, 1 table) — a short position/framework paper, not a substantive survey. Crucially, it treats classical decentralized multi-agent systems broadly rather than LLM-based agents specifically. It does not organize fairness by the LLM agent pipeline components (tool selection, memory/RAG, multi-agent delegation, planning, user modeling, long-horizon drift). It has no action-level disparity metrics, no evaluation framework, no empirical audit, no mitigation map by component, and no consequential-domain grounding. Our survey is 60+ pages with 127 cited papers, original cross-framework audit, and a complete evaluation + mitigation framework.

papers.title: A Survey on Reliability, Transparency, Accountability, and Fairness in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems through the Responsibility Lens
papers.authors: Sana Ebrahimi, Abolfazl Asudeh
papers.venue: Preprint (ResearchGate/unpublished), University of Illinois Chicago
papers.overlap: 39-page survey examining LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems through four pillars: Reliability, Transparency, Accountability, and Fairness and Ethics. Covers coordination patterns (debate, critique, aggregation, routing), governance, logging, and fairness mechanisms across 60+ works from 2023-2025.
papers.how_ours_differs: Fairness is one of four RTAF pillars, not the organizing axis. The paper covers LLM-MAS broadly through a responsibility lens; it does not decompose fairness by the six agent components (tool selection, memory, delegation, planning, user modeling, long-horizon), does not propose action-level disparity metrics, and does not include an empirical audit. No arXiv ID found — not indexable as a competing arXiv preprint. Our survey provides the dedicated component-organized fairness taxonomy that this paper's fairness pillar sketches only briefly.

papers.title: Interactional Fairness in LLM Multi-Agent Systems: An Evaluation Framework
papers.authors: Ruta Binkyte
papers.venue: AAAI/ACM AIES 2025 (Proceedings, Vol. 8, pp. 457-468)
papers.overlap: Introduces interactional fairness evaluation (interpersonal + informational fairness) for LLM multi-agent negotiation tasks, drawing on organizational psychology. Addresses how agent communication tone and justification quality affect fairness outcomes.
papers.how_ours_differs: Narrow scope: covers only inter-agent communication fairness in resource negotiation, not demographic/protected-group fairness in LLM agents. Does not address tool selection, memory/RAG, planning, user modeling, or long-horizon bias. The fairness concept is organizational-justice fairness (procedural/interactional), not group/individual/counterfactual fairness. Not a survey. Orthogonal to our component-organized demographic bias framework.

papers.title: Emergent Bias and Fairness in Multi-Agent Decision Systems
papers.authors: Maeve Madigan, Parameswaran Kamalaruban, Glenn Moynihan, Tom Kempton, David Sutton, Stuart Burrell
papers.venue: arXiv preprint arXiv:2512.16433
papers.overlap: Empirically investigates fairness risks in multi-agent systems for financial decisions (credit scoring, income estimation). Demonstrates that bias patterns cannot be traced to individual agent components and that the collective can be more or less biased than members — relevant to our §3.7 agentic multiplier argument. This paper is already cited in our draft as madigan2025emergent.
papers.how_ours_differs: Not a survey. Narrow domain (financial tabular). Its key finding — bias patterns cannot be reduced to individual components — is an empirical result we cite as evidence for the need for trajectory-level evaluation, one of our open-agenda points. Our survey synthesizes this and related work into a broader component taxonomy, not a single-domain simulation study.

papers.title: Responsible Agentic Reasoning and AI Agents: A Critical Survey
papers.authors: Shaina Raza, Ranjan Sapkota, Manoj Karkee, Christos Emmanouilidis
papers.venue: TechRxiv / IEEE preprint (DOI: 10.36227/techrxiv.175735299.97215847)
papers.overlap: Surveys responsible agentic AI (R2A2 framework) covering reasoning methods (CoT, ReAct, tree-of-thought, tool use, memory, RAG, agentic browsing) and responsible AI principles (fairness, privacy, transparency, accountability). Covers studies from January 2023 to November 2025.
papers.how_ours_differs: Organizing axis is responsible reasoning (fairness + privacy + safety + transparency + accountability together), not fairness alone. Fairness is one of five responsible AI dimensions. Does not organize by agent component from a fairness perspective, does not propose action-level disparity metrics, does not include an empirical audit or domain-specific harm evidence (hiring, lending, healthcare). Not indexed on arXiv. Our survey is fairness-dedicated and component-organized.

papers.title: TRiSM for Agentic AI: A Review of Trust, Risk, and Security Management in LLM-based Agentic Multi-Agent Systems
papers.authors: Shaina Raza, Ranjan Sapkota, Manoj Karkee, Christos Emmanouilidis
papers.venue: arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.04133
papers.overlap: Reviews Trust, Risk, and Security Management in LLM-based multi-agent systems, with five pillars: Explainability, ModelOps, Security, Privacy, Lifecycle Governance. Proposes two novel metrics (Component Synergy Score, Tool Utilization Efficacy).
papers.how_ours_differs: Does not cover fairness/equity/bias as a dedicated topic — fairness is absent from the five TRiSM pillars. The organizing axis is security/risk management, not demographic fairness. No connection to group/individual/counterfactual fairness frameworks or action-level disparity. Distinct from our work.

papers.title: Towards trustworthy agentic AI: a comprehensive survey of safety, robustness, privacy, and system security
papers.authors: Jinhu Qi, Muzhi Li, Jiahong Liu, Yuqin Shu, Dianzhi Yu, Shicheng Ma, Wenqian Cui, Yiyang Zhao, Yiyi Chen, Ruoxi Jiang, Irwin King, Zenglin Xu
papers.venue: arXiv preprint arXiv:2605.23989
papers.overlap: Comprehensive survey of trustworthy agentic AI through two core pillars: Safety/Robustness and Privacy/System Security. Mentions fairness and accountability explicitly as 'relevant context rather than parallel chapters.'
papers.how_ours_differs: Fairness is explicitly secondary — the authors themselves state fairness is discussed as context, not a parallel chapter. The organizing axis is safety + privacy/security threats. No component-organized fairness analysis, no action-level disparity metrics, no empirical fairness audit. Distinct from our work.

papers.title: Towards a framework for Bias Evaluation of AI Agents in Agentic Workflows
papers.authors: Unknown (ACM proceedings)
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2026 Conference on Human Centred Artificial Intelligence — Education and Practice (ACM DL DOI: 10.1145/3777490.3777504)
papers.overlap: Proposes an evaluation framework for bias in agentic workflows using demographic swapping, contamination prevention, and statistical analysis for multi-agent workflows. Reports bias amplification factors up to 1.51x in sequential agent interactions. Addresses EU AI Act Article 10 compliance.
papers.how_ours_differs: Framework paper focused on evaluation methodology for multi-stage agentic bias (amplification in workflows), not a survey. Does not cover the full spectrum of agent components, does not propose CFR/MASD or action-level metrics, and is primarily a compliance/audit tool rather than an organizing taxonomy of where bias enters the agent pipeline. Not a broad survey; no arXiv ID found.

papers.title: MALIBU Benchmark: Multi-Agent LLM Implicit Bias Uncovered
papers.authors: Imran Mirza, Cole Huang, Ishwara Vasista, Rohan Patil, Asli Akalin, Sean O'Brien, Kevin Zhu
papers.venue: Building Trust in LLMs @ ICLR 2025; NAACL SRW 2025 (arXiv:2507.01019)
papers.overlap: Benchmark paper uncovering implicit bias in multi-agent LLM assignment scenarios using demographic personas (gender, race, religion). Shows multi-agent systems can reproduce and amplify biases. Cited in our draft as mirza2025malibu.
papers.how_ours_differs: Not a survey. Covers one agent component (multi-agent role/task assignment) and only the persona-induced bias dimension. Does not cover tool selection, memory/RAG, planning, user modeling, or long-horizon bias. Our survey situates MALIBU as evidence within the multi-agent delegation component of a broader component taxonomy.

papers.title: Fairness in Multi-Agent Systems for Software Engineering: An SDLC-Oriented Rapid Review
papers.authors: Corey Yang-Smith, Ronnie de Souza Santos, Ahmad Abdellatif
papers.venue: LLMTrust Workshop at FSE Companion 2026 (arXiv:2604.13103)
papers.overlap: Rapid review (18 retained from 350) of fairness in LLM multi-agent systems for software development. Characterizes fairness through trustworthy AI principles, bias reduction, and interactional dynamics. Identifies gaps: fragmented evaluation, limited generalization, scarce governance.
papers.how_ours_differs: Domain-restricted to software engineering; rapid review methodology (only 18 papers); does not organize by agent component (tool selection, memory, planning, user modeling, long-horizon); does not propose action-level disparity metrics or an evaluation framework. Workshop paper. Our survey is cross-domain, 127 papers, with a full component taxonomy and evaluation/mitigation frameworks.

papers.title: Bias and Unfairness in Information Retrieval Systems: New Challenges in the LLM Era
papers.authors: Sunhao Dai, Chen Xu, Shicheng Xu, Liang Pang, Zhenhua Dong, Jun Xu
papers.venue: KDD 2024 Tutorial & Survey; also WSDM 2025 tutorial (arXiv:2404.11457)
papers.overlap: Surveys fairness/bias in IR systems augmented with LLMs (search, reranking, RAG as a retrieval pipeline). Covers our §3.2 Memory and Retrieval component partially, since RAG is a retrieval system.
papers.how_ours_differs: Covers static IR systems (search engines, recommenders), not autonomous LLM agents. Does not address tool selection, multi-agent delegation, planning, user modeling, or long-horizon agent behavior. Framing is retrieval-system bias, not agent-component bias. Our survey is the agent-side complement to this IR-side survey.

papers.title: Navigating the Risks: A Survey of Security, Privacy, and Ethics Threats in LLM-Based Agents
papers.authors: Yuyou Gan, Yong Yang, Zhe Ma, Ping He, Rui Zeng, Yiming Wang, Qingming Li, Chunyi Zhou, Songze Li, Ting Wang, Yunjun Gao, Yingcai Wu, Shouling Ji
papers.venue: arXiv preprint arXiv:2411.09523
papers.overlap: Surveys security, privacy, and ethics threats in LLM agents via a taxonomy based on sources and impacts. Ethics section includes bias. Notes that bias in LLM-based agents involves the LLM-based controller and multi-modal inputs/outputs.
papers.how_ours_differs: Organizing axis is security/privacy/ethics threats, not fairness. Bias receives brief treatment within a broad 'ethics' category alongside many other threat types. No component-organized fairness taxonomy, no action-level metrics, no empirical fairness audit. Our survey goes substantially deeper into each fairness-relevant component with dedicated mechanisms, evidence, and measurement instruments.

papers.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents
papers.authors: Asaf Yehudai, Lilach Eden, Alan Li, Guy Uziel, Yilun Zhao, Roy Bar-Haim, Arman Cohan, Michal Shmueli-Scheuer
papers.venue: arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.16416 (revised April 2026)
papers.overlap: Comprehensive survey of LLM agent evaluation through five perspectives: core capabilities, application-specific benchmarks, generalist evaluation, benchmark dimensions, and evaluation frameworks. Mentions bias mitigation as a gap in current benchmarks.
papers.how_ours_differs: Not focused on fairness at all — fairness/bias appears as an explicitly identified gap ('evaluations still lack comprehensive tests for bias mitigation'). The organizational axis is evaluation methodology, not fairness mechanisms. Our survey fills precisely the gap this paper calls out.

result.threat_assessment: No found paper genuinely scoops the core novelty claim of our survey: a dedicated, agent-component-organized fairness taxonomy of LLM-based agents covering all six components (tool/API selection, memory and retrieval, multi-agent delegation, planning and decomposition, user modeling, and long-horizon drift), paired with a generalized evaluation framework (CFR/MASD/action-level disparity), a mitigation map, and an original empirical audit. The closest candidates and honest assessments follow. arXiv:2502.07254 (Ranjan et al., Feb 2025) is the most title-similar paper — it self-describes as 'a comprehensive survey of fairness in multi-agent AI.' However, it is only 12 pages and 1 table, treats classical decentralized multi-agent systems broadly (not LLM-based agents specifically), proposes no component-organized taxonomy, offers no action-level metrics or empirical audit, and does not cite the LLM agent literature (ReAct, Toolformer, AutoGen, RAG, etc.). It is a position/framework sketch, not a substantive survey — a CSUR reviewer would not consider it overlapping at publication depth. The Ebrahimi-Asudeh RTAF survey (2025, ResearchGate-only) covers fairness as one of four pillars in LLM-MAS; at 39 pages it is the most structurally similar work, but fairness is one pillar among four (reliability, transparency, accountability, fairness), not the organizing axis. No arXiv ID is discoverable, suggesting it has not been submitted to major venues. Our survey's fairness-only organization, six-component taxonomy, evaluation framework, and empirical audit are substantially more comprehensive on the fairness dimension than this paper's single pillar. All other found papers are either (a) broad trustworthiness surveys where fairness is one secondary dimension (arXiv:2503.09648 TrustAgent; arXiv:2605.23989 Qi et al.; arXiv:2506.04133 TRiSM; arXiv:2507.21504 Mohammadi et al.), (b) single-domain empirical papers not surveys (arXiv:2602.14970 Mayilvaghanan; arXiv:2512.16433 Madigan et al.; arXiv:2507.01019 MALIBU), (c) domain-restricted rapid reviews (arXiv:2604.13103 Yang-Smith et al. — software engineering only), (d) IR-focused not agent-focused (arXiv:2404.11457 Dai et al.), or (e) healthcare-specific with fairness as one of seven dimensions (arXiv:2602.04813 Vatsal et al.). The CSUR reviewers' most natural challenge will be arXiv:2502.07254 and the Ebrahimi-Asudeh RTAF survey; the response is already written into our §1.4 (Positioning) — both treat fairness as one dimension or use a classical-MAS rather than LLM-pipeline framing, and neither delivers component-organized analysis at survey depth. The originality claim of 'first dedicated survey of fairness in LLM-based agents organized by agent component' holds as of June 2026.

papers.title: Fairness in Large Language Models: A Taxonomic Survey
papers.authors: Zhibo Chu, Zichong Wang, Wenbin Zhang
papers.venue: ACM SIGKDD Explorations Newsletter, vol. 26(1), pp. 34-48
papers.overlap: DIRECT PARENT LINEAGE. Taxonomizes bias in LLMs that answer — covers intrinsic/extrinsic bias, fairness definitions (group, individual, counterfactual), and debiasing of LLM generation. Senior author Wenbin Zhang is also our senior author. Our paper explicitly positions itself as 'the agentic successor' to this work.
papers.how_ours_differs: Chu et al. treat LLMs as static generators; our survey treats LLMs as components of acting pipelines (tool selection, memory, multi-agent delegation, planning, user modeling, long-horizon drift). Their measurement axis is token-level output; ours is action-level disparity. We inherit their definitional grounding and extend every dimension to the agentic surface they do not treat.

papers.title: A Survey on Bias and Fairness in Machine Learning
papers.authors: Ninareh Mehrabi, Fred Morstatter, Naveen Saxena, Kristina Lerman, Aram Galstyan
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 54, no. 6, pp. 1-35
papers.overlap: Foundational fairness survey cataloguing 20+ bias sources in classical ML pipeline (pre/in/post-processing). Our §2.1 explicitly uses their taxonomy as the 'baseline vocabulary' and extends it upstream into agent components. Widely cited by CSUR reviewers as the canonical ML fairness reference.
papers.how_ours_differs: Mehrabi et al. cover classical ML pipelines only, with no LLM or agent coverage. We extend their taxonomy to the six agent-pipeline components that did not exist when they wrote. The reviewer expectation is engagement, which we provide explicitly in §2.1.

papers.title: Fairness in Machine Learning: A Survey
papers.authors: Simon Caton, Christian Haas
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 56, no. 7, Article 144
papers.overlap: CSUR survey organising ML fairness into pre/in/post-processing. Covers group fairness, individual fairness, and mitigation. No LLM or agent content. Likely cited by CSUR reviewers as adjacent CSUR prior art our survey should engage.
papers.how_ours_differs: Caton and Haas scope to classical supervised learning. Ours is entirely focused on LLM-based agents and the new fairness failure modes (tool routing, RAG injection, role assignment, long-horizon drift) that their pre/in/post taxonomy cannot capture. We cite them for definitional grounding only.

papers.title: A Review on Fairness in Machine Learning
papers.authors: Dana Pessach, Eytan Shmueli
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 3, Article 51
papers.overlap: Another CSUR fairness-in-ML survey covering causes of algorithmic bias and common group/individual fairness measures, focusing on classification tasks. CSUR reviewers will expect engagement with this survey as prior CSUR art.
papers.how_ours_differs: Pessach and Shmueli scope to classification and prediction, not to generation or agency. The agent-pipeline loci we taxonomize (tool selection, multi-agent delegation, etc.) are entirely outside their scope.

papers.title: What-is and How-to for Fairness in Machine Learning: A Survey, Reflection, and Perspective
papers.authors: Zeyu Tang, Jiji Zhang, Kun Zhang
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 13s, Article 299
papers.overlap: CSUR survey linking ML fairness notions to moral/political philosophy (theories of justice) and addressing long-term impact of decisions. The long-term fairness angle touches our §3.6 on long-horizon drift.
papers.how_ours_differs: Tang et al. discuss long-term impact abstractly in a classical ML framing. We operationalize long-term fairness in LLM agents empirically — multi-turn degradation (FairMT-Bench), sycophancy drift, feedback loops — with agent-specific metrics that their philosophical framing cannot capture.

papers.title: Fairness in Ranking, Part I: Score-Based Ranking
papers.authors: Meike Zehlike, Ke Yang, Julia Stoyanovich
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 6, Article 118
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on fairness in score-based ranking algorithms. Directly relevant to our §3.1 (tool/API selection bias) and §3.2 (retrieval bias), where ranking fairness determines which tools or documents are prioritized. CSUR reviewers will expect engagement.
papers.how_ours_differs: Zehlike et al. treat fairness as a property of a static ranking algorithm. We situate retrieval and tool ranking inside an agent pipeline where the bias compounds across subsequent steps (the agentic multiplier, §3.7). We also treat the ranker as one of six pipeline loci rather than as the entire system.

papers.title: Fairness in Ranking, Part II: Learning-to-Rank and Recommender Systems
papers.authors: Meike Zehlike, Ke Yang, Julia Stoyanovich
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 6, Article 119
papers.overlap: CSUR companion to Part I, extending to learning-to-rank and recommender systems. Directly relevant to §3.5 (user modeling and personalization harms) and our treatment of LLM-based recommenders.
papers.how_ours_differs: Zehlike et al. cover pre-LLM recommender fairness. We cover LLM-native agents that use recommendation logic alongside tool calling, memory, and planning — where personalization harms arise from inferred user models, not explicit recommendation scores. Their metrics do not apply to action-level decisions.

papers.title: A Survey on the Fairness of Recommender Systems
papers.authors: Yifan Wang, Weizhi Ma, Min Zhang, Yiqun Liu, Shaoping Ma
papers.venue: ACM Transactions on Information Systems, vol. 41, no. 2, Article 38
papers.overlap: ACM TOIS survey on fairness across consumer, provider, and platform stakeholders in recommender systems. Relevant to §3.5 (personalization harms) and the CFaiRLLM paper we cite.
papers.how_ours_differs: Wang et al. cover classical recommender systems without LLM backbone. We cover LLM-based agents whose 'recommendation' is embedded in a broader action pipeline with tool use, memory, and multi-agent coordination. Their stakeholder framework is useful for consumer fairness (our §3.5) but not for the other five components.

papers.title: Trustworthy AI: From Principles to Practices
papers.authors: Bo Li, Peng Qi, Bo Liu, Shuai Di, Jingen Liu, Jiquan Pei, Jinfeng Yi, Bowen Zhou
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 9, Article 177
papers.overlap: CSUR survey covering the full trustworthy AI stack: robustness, generalization, explainability, transparency, fairness, privacy, and accountability. Fairness is one of seven+ dimensions; no agentic or LLM-agent content. CSUR reviewers will expect engagement as prior CSUR art.
papers.how_ours_differs: Li et al. treat fairness as one dimension among many in a broad trustworthy-AI review covering the full model lifecycle. We take fairness as our sole organizing axis and drill into the agentic pipeline loci that their high-level framework cannot distinguish. They do not cover tool selection, multi-agent delegation, RAG bias, or long-horizon drift.

papers.title: Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence: A Review
papers.authors: Davinder Kaur, Suleyman Uslu, Kaley J. Rittichier, Arjan Durresi
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 2, Article 39
papers.overlap: Earlier CSUR trustworthy AI survey covering fairness, explainability, accountability, reliability. No LLM or agent content. Provides the trustworthy-AI vocabulary that CSUR reviewers will expect engagement with.
papers.how_ours_differs: Kaur et al. predate the LLM agent era and address trustworthiness at the model/system level. Our scope is specifically LLM-based agents and the agent-component loci where fairness fails in ways their framework cannot see.

papers.title: Towards Trustworthy AI: A Review of Ethical and Robust Large Language Models
papers.authors: Md Meftahul Ferdaus, Mahdi Abdelguerfi, Elias Ioup, Kendall N. Niles, Ken Pathak, Steven Sloan
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 7
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on ethical and robust LLMs covering unintended harms (including bias/fairness), transparency, adversarial vulnerability, value alignment, and environmental impact. Covers fairness in LLMs. Does NOT cover agentic systems or agent-pipeline components.
papers.how_ours_differs: Ferdaus et al. review trustworthiness of LLMs as text generators across five ethical dimensions; they do not analyse the agent pipeline. Our distinct contribution is the per-component fairness taxonomy across the six agentic loci (tool selection, memory, delegation, planning, user modeling, long-horizon drift) that arise only when LLMs are embedded in acting systems.

papers.title: The Emerged Security and Privacy of LLM Agent: A Survey with Case Studies
papers.authors: Feng He, Tianqing Zhu, Dayong Ye, Bo Liu, Wanlei Zhou, Philip S. Yu
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 6
papers.overlap: CSUR survey specifically on LLM agents, organized around security and privacy threats and defenses across agent components (brain, memory, tool, user, agent, environment). Covers the same six-component agent anatomy we use but through a security/privacy lens.
papers.how_ours_differs: He et al. organize by threat type (privacy leakage, adversarial attacks, jailbreaking) not by fairness. They do not cover demographic disparity, group/individual/counterfactual fairness metrics, or action-level disparity. Our survey is the fairness-axis complement to their security-axis survey — sharing the agent-component anatomy but bringing an entirely different analytical lens and a different body of literature.

papers.title: A Survey on the Optimization of Large Language Model-based Agents
papers.authors: Shangheng Du, Jiabao Zhao, Jinxin Shi, Zhentao Xie, Xin Jiang, Yanhong Bai, Liang He
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 9, Article 223
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on LLM agent optimization covering parameter-driven (fine-tuning, RL) and parameter-free (prompt engineering, retrieval) methods. Agent components covered include planning, memory, tool use. No fairness content.
papers.how_ours_differs: Du et al. optimise for performance (task success, efficiency). We optimise for fairness (demographic parity, counterfactual invariance) — a fundamentally different target that requires different metrics, evaluation protocols, and mitigation strategies. The optimization approaches they review (RL, fine-tuning) appear in our §5 as one mitigation family among several.

papers.title: Generalizability of Large Language Model-Based Agents: A Comprehensive Survey
papers.authors: Minxing Zhang, Yi Yang, Roy Xie, Bhuwan Dhingra, Shuyan Zhou, Jian Pei
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 10, Article 263
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on LLM agent generalizability — consistent performance across varied instructions, tasks, environments, and domains. Categorizes methods targeting the LLM backbone, agent components, or LLM-component interaction. No fairness content.
papers.how_ours_differs: Zhang et al. define generalizability as performance consistency; we define fairness as outcome parity across demographic groups. These are orthogonal goals: a fully generalisable agent can still route protected groups to inferior tools. We cite this CSUR article to show the agent-evaluation space and position our fairness-specific evaluation framework (§4) as complementary.

papers.title: LLLMs: A Data-Driven Survey of Evolving Research on Limitations of Large Language Models
papers.authors: Aida Kostikova, Zhipin Wang, Deidamea Bajri, Ole Putz, Benjamin Paassen, Steffen Eger
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, published April 17, 2026
papers.overlap: CSUR data-driven meta-survey of 14,648 papers on LLM limitations (2022-2025), covering reasoning, generalization, hallucination, bias, and security. Bias/fairness is the fourth most studied limitation category.
papers.how_ours_differs: Kostikova et al. survey LLM limitations at the static-model level with bibliometric methods; their bias category covers QA-era findings. Our survey is scoped exclusively to the agentic surface — the bias failures that emerge only when LLMs are deployed as acting agents — which is not represented as a distinct category in their corpus.

papers.title: A Survey on Evaluation of Large Language Models
papers.authors: Yupeng Chang, Xu Wang, Jindong Wang, Yuan Wu, Linyi Yang, Kaijie Zhu, Hao Chen, Xiaoyuan Yi, Cunxiang Wang, Yidong Wang, Wei Ye, Yue Zhang, Yi Chang, Philip S. Yu, Qiang Yang, Xing Xie
papers.venue: ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology (TIST), vol. 15, no. 3, Article 39
papers.overlap: Major ACM TIST survey on LLM evaluation covering what to evaluate (knowledge, reasoning, fairness, safety), where to evaluate (datasets, benchmarks), and how to evaluate. Fairness is one evaluation dimension. No agentic evaluation framework.
papers.how_ours_differs: Chang et al. evaluate LLMs as text generators; fairness is one of many capabilities to benchmark. Our §4 constructs an evaluation framework specifically for acting agents — group, individual, and counterfactual fairness lifted to trajectory-level outcomes and action-level disparity metrics — that their general survey does not provide.

papers.title: Evaluation and Benchmarking of LLM Agents: A Survey
papers.authors: Mahmoud Mohammadi, Yipeng Li, Jane Lo, Wendy Yip
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 31st ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD '25)
papers.overlap: NEAR-NEIGHBOR explicitly cited in our positioning (§1.4 and §3). Broad agent-evaluation survey where fairness is one sub-topic among many (task completion, safety, robustness, efficiency). Two-dimensional taxonomy organizes evaluation objectives without making fairness the central axis.
papers.how_ours_differs: Mohammadi et al. treat fairness as one sub-topic in a general agent-evaluation survey. Our survey takes fairness as the sole organizing axis and achieves substantially deeper coverage of each component's bias mechanisms, measurement instruments (CFR, MASD, action-level disparity), and mitigation strategies. This differentiation is explicitly argued in our §1.4 and §3.

papers.title: A Survey on Trustworthy LLM Agents: Threats and Countermeasures
papers.authors: Miao Yu, Fanci Meng, Xinyun Zhou, Shilong Wang, Junyuan Mao, Linsey Pang, Tianlong Chen, Kun Wang, Xinfeng Li, Yongfeng Zhang, Bo An, Qingsong Wen
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 31st ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD '25)
papers.overlap: Trustworthiness survey of LLM agents organized around intrinsic (brain, memory, tool) and extrinsic (user, agent, environment) aspects. Covers safety, truthfulness, robustness, and some fairness. Uses similar agent-anatomy decomposition to ours.
papers.how_ours_differs: Yu et al. address trustworthiness broadly with fairness as one consideration among many trust properties. We are fairness-dedicated, providing the per-component bias mechanisms, counterfactual evaluation methods (CFR, MASD), and action-level disparity metrics that their general trustworthiness framework does not develop. Their threat taxonomy (adversarial, jailbreak, hallucination) differs from our fairness taxonomy (demographic disparity, compounding bias, personalization harm).

papers.title: Bias and Unfairness in Information Retrieval Systems: New Challenges in the LLM Era
papers.authors: Sunhao Dai, Chen Xu, Shicheng Xu, Liang Pang, Zhenhua Dong, Jun Xu
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 30th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD '24)
papers.overlap: Survey and framework for bias and unfairness in LLM-integrated IR systems. Covers how LLM integration introduces new bias challenges at retrieval, ranking, and generation stages. Directly relevant to our §3.2 (memory and retrieval bias). Cited in our draft as 'dai2024bias'.
papers.how_ours_differs: Dai et al. focus on the IR pipeline (query, retrieval, ranking, generation) as an end-to-end system. We situate retrieval as one of six agent components and analyse how retrieval-injected bias compounds with bias from tool selection, multi-agent delegation, and planning — the cross-component compounding analysis is absent from their IR-centric scope.

papers.title: Actions Speak Louder than Words: Agent Decisions Reveal Implicit Biases in Language Models
papers.authors: Yuxuan Li, Hirokazu Shirado, Sauvik Das
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2025 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT '25)
papers.overlap: FAccT paper showing that agent decision-making reveals implicit biases invisible to QA-level evaluation — the empirical anchor of our measurement-gap thesis. Cited extensively in our draft as 'li2025actions'. Proposes simulating agents with sociodemographic personas and measuring decision disparities.
papers.how_ours_differs: Li et al. demonstrate the measurement gap in one experimental setting (persona-based agent simulations in specific decision scenarios). Our survey generalises this finding across all six agent components, provides the formal action-level disparity metrics (§4.3), and situates the result in the broader taxonomy. We do not compete with this paper — we build the survey framework on top of it.

papers.title: Visibility into AI Agents
papers.authors: Alan Chan, Carson Ezell, Max Kaufmann, Kevin Wei, Lewis Hammond, Herbie Bradley, Emma Bluemke, Nitarshan Rajkumar, David Krueger, Noam Kolt, Lennart Heim, Markus Anderljung
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2024 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT '24)
papers.overlap: FAccT position paper arguing that delegation of complex goals to AI agents exacerbates societal risks. Proposes transparency mechanisms: unique agent tags, real-time monitoring, activity logging. Touches accountability infrastructure relevant to our §8 research agenda.
papers.how_ours_differs: Chan et al. address AI agent visibility and governance mechanisms (what information should be disclosed). We address fairness and equity (whether agents produce disparate outcomes across demographic groups) — adjacent but orthogonal concerns. We cite this for the accountability framing in our research agenda.

papers.title: Towards a framework for Bias Evaluation of AI Agents in Agentic Workflows
papers.authors: Unknown (ACM HCAIep '26 proceedings)
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2026 Conference on Human Centred Artificial Intelligence - Education and Practice (HCAIep '26)
papers.overlap: Presents a bias evaluation framework for multi-agent workflows with demographic swapping methodology and statistical analysis of bias evolution across sequential agent interactions. Directly overlaps our §4 (evaluation) and §3.7 (agentic multiplier).
papers.how_ours_differs: This paper presents one evaluation framework for one workflow setting. Our survey taxonomizes bias across all six agent components, covers the full landscape of evaluation methods (CFR, MASD, action-level disparity, group/individual/counterfactual families), provides a mitigation map, and delivers the comprehensive component-organized taxonomy that this single-paper contribution does not. We should cite and position explicitly relative to it.

papers.title: Software Fairness: An Analysis and Survey
papers.authors: Ezekiel Soremekun, Mike Papadakis, Maxime Cordy, Yves Le Traon
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 3 (October 2025)
papers.overlap: CSUR survey analysing 164 publications on fairness as a software engineering property, covering specification, testing, and mitigation of bias in learning-based software. Mentions intersectional and sequential fairness as open challenges.
papers.how_ours_differs: Soremekun et al. treat fairness as a software property of ML systems in general, with emphasis on testing and verification methods. They do not cover LLM agents, agentic architectures, or the agent-component loci we taxonomize. Their 'sequential fairness' mention is an open challenge in their framework; we address it in depth in §3.6 (long-horizon drift) and §4.

papers.title: Bias and Fairness in Large Language Models: A Survey
papers.authors: Isabel O. Gallegos, Ryan A. Rossi, Joe Barrow, Md Mehrab Tanjim, Sungchul Kim, Franck Dernoncourt, Tong Yu, Ruiyi Zhang, Nesreen K. Ahmed
papers.venue: Computational Linguistics, vol. 50, no. 3, pp. 1097-1179 (MIT Press / ACL)
papers.overlap: Authoritative survey of bias evaluation and mitigation in LLMs covering stereotyping, toxicity, representational harm, and distributional disparities in generated text. Explicitly cited in our §2.2 as 'gallegos2024bias' — the primary reference for the QA-era bias literature we depart from.
papers.how_ours_differs: Gallegos et al. survey bias in static LLM generation (single-turn). Our survey is organized by agent component, not by bias type. Our §2.2 explicitly states their work 'ably synthesizes' the generation literature we exclude from our scope. We differentiate by focusing on the agentic surface that their survey does not address.

papers.title: Preserving the Fairness Guarantees of Classifiers in Changing Environments: A Survey
papers.authors: A. Barrainkua, P. Gordaliza, J. A. Lozano, N. Quadrianto
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 57, no. 6, pp. 1-31
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on fair classification under distribution shift — maintaining fairness when training and test distributions differ. Covers temporal and covariate shift. Tangentially relevant to our §3.6 (long-horizon drift) where deployment feedback loops shift data distributions.
papers.how_ours_differs: Barrainkua et al. address static classifier fairness under distributional shift. We address dynamic agent fairness under self-induced feedback loops where the agent's own outputs become future inputs (performative loops, §3.6). Their distribution-shift framing is useful background but our agentic setting has distinct mechanism and remediation needs.

papers.title: Triangular Trade-off between Robustness, Accuracy, and Fairness in Deep Neural Networks: A Survey
papers.authors: J. Li, G. Li
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 57, no. 6, Article 140
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on the three-way trade-off between accuracy, robustness, and fairness in deep networks. Relevant as background for our §5 (mitigation) discussion of the safety-utility trade-off in debiasing.
papers.how_ours_differs: Li and Li address the trade-off in static DNN training. Our survey addresses mitigation in acting agents where the trade-off is more complex: component-level interventions can resolve a fairness problem in one pipeline stage while introducing it in another. Their three-way framing does not account for cross-component compounding (§3.7).

papers.title: Towards a Better Understanding of Evaluating Trustworthiness in AI Systems
papers.authors: Nils Kemmerzell, Annika Schreiner, Haroon Khalid, Michael Schalk, Letizia Bordoli
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 57, pp. 1-38
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on evaluation strategies for trustworthy AI, deriving an evaluation framework for operationalizing trustworthiness. Fairness is one of the trustworthiness dimensions evaluated. No specific LLM-agent content.
papers.how_ours_differs: Kemmerzell et al. provide a general trustworthiness evaluation framework. Our paper contributes the fairness-specific evaluation infrastructure for LLM-based agents: trajectory-level counterfactual fairness, action-level disparity metrics (CFR, MASD), and component-granular measurement — none of which are covered in their general trustworthiness framework.

papers.title: Trustworthy Information Retrieval in the LLM Era: Bias, Unfairness, and Hallucination
papers.authors: Sunhao Dai, Changle Qu, Sirui Chen, Xiao Zhang, Jun Xu
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2025 Annual International ACM SIGIR Conference (SIGIR-AP '25)
papers.overlap: ACM SIGIR tutorial unifying bias, unfairness, and hallucination in LLM-era IR under a distribution-mismatch framework. Directly relevant to our §3.2 (retrieval bias) and §3.1 (tool selection as an IR problem).
papers.how_ours_differs: Dai et al. frame IR trustworthiness as distribution alignment within a retrieval pipeline. We situate retrieval as one of six agent components and focus on demographic fairness specifically — a narrower but deeper treatment that includes counterfactual evaluation methods and action-level disparity not present in their broader trustworthiness framing.

papers.title: Fairness in Multi-Agent Systems for Software Engineering: An SDLC-Oriented Rapid Review
papers.authors: Corey Yang-Smith, Ronnie de Souza Santos, Ahmad Abdellatif
papers.venue: arXiv preprint (April 2026), not yet ACM-published
papers.overlap: Rapid review (18 retained studies from 350) on fairness in LLM-enabled multi-agent systems for software engineering, organized by SDLC phase. Most directly overlapping our §3.3 (multi-agent delegation bias).
papers.how_ours_differs: Yang-Smith et al. scope to software engineering tasks and retain only 18 studies. Our survey covers all consequential domains (hiring, healthcare, lending, education), retains 127 papers, and provides the full six-component taxonomy with evaluation and mitigation frameworks. Their SE-specific focus is a proper subset of our coverage.

papers.title: Towards trustworthy agentic AI: a comprehensive survey of safety, robustness, privacy, and system security
papers.authors: Jinhu Qi, Muzhi Li, Jiahong Liu, Yuqin Shu, Dianzhi Yu, Shicheng Ma, Wenqian Cui, and others
papers.venue: arXiv preprint 2605.23989 (May 2026)
papers.overlap: Comprehensive survey on trustworthy agentic AI organized along Safety/Robustness and Privacy/System Security dimensions, decomposed by the five-stage agent lifecycle (Perceive-Plan-Act-Reflect-Learn). Notes fairness and accountability as adjacent dimensions but does not treat them.
papers.how_ours_differs: Qi et al. focus on safety, robustness, privacy, and security; fairness is explicitly listed as outside their scope. Our survey is the fairness complement to their safety/security survey. We share the agent-lifecycle anatomy but bring an entirely different measurement and mitigation apparatus for demographic disparity.

result.threat_assessment: No paper found substantially scoops our dedicated agent-component fairness survey. The field contains: (1) QA-era LLM fairness surveys (Gallegos et al. 2024; Chu et al. 2024) that do not cover the agentic surface; (2) general agent-evaluation surveys (Mohammadi et al. KDD '25; Yu et al. KDD '25) where fairness is one sub-topic among many with no component-organized taxonomy; (3) adjacent ACM CSUR surveys on trustworthy AI (Li et al. 2023; He et al. 2025; Ferdaus et al. 2026) that cover agent anatomy or LLM ethics but not demographic fairness organized by agent component; (4) fairness-in-ML CSUR surveys (Mehrabi 2021; Caton 2024; Pessach 2022; Tang 2023) focused on classical supervised learning. The single closest candidate is the small-scope arXiv rapid review on multi-agent SE fairness (Yang-Smith et al. 2026, 18 papers, SE domain only), which does not threaten novelty. The FAccT 2026 workshop paper (DOI 10.1145/3777490.3777504) on bias evaluation frameworks for agentic workflows is an empirical single-paper contribution, not a survey, and lacks the full taxonomy, mitigation map, and evaluation framework our survey delivers. Confirmed: as of June 2026, no dedicated, agent-component-organized, cross-domain fairness survey of LLM-based agents exists in ACM venues or arXiv. Our novelty claim ('first dedicated taxonomy of fairness in LLM-based agents organized by agent component') stands.

result.result:
# CSUR Review: "Fairness and Equity in LLM-Based Agents: A Taxonomic Survey"

**Recommendation: Major Revision (borderline Reject-and-Resubmit in current form).** The organizing idea is publishable and the coverage matrix (§7) is genuinely good, but the draft has one fatal internal contradiction, several honesty/positioning problems, a taxonomy with unresolved category errors, and substantial stretches of annotated-bibliography prose. It also still wears its SIGKDD Explorations clothing — at ~15–20pp with 127 corpus papers, it is roughly half the length and depth CSUR expects.

---

## 1. Originality of the framework & quality of synthesis

**The framework is original in combination, not in parts — and the paper never confronts this.** The six "components" are the standard agent anatomy from Wang/Xi/Sumers (planning, memory, tool use, action, persona), crossed with fairness. That cross is a legitimate contribution, but a hostile reviewer will say: "you took the agent-survey decomposition and ran a fairness literature search per box." The defense must be that the *mechanisms of harm* differ per component in ways that demand different metrics and mitigations — the paper asserts this repeatedly but demonstrates it unevenly (strong for tools/multi-agent, weak for planning).

**The taxonomy has category errors a CSUR reviewer will not forgive:**

- **§3.6 "Long-horizon drift" is not a component; it is a temporal property of the other five.** You have five architectural loci plus one dynamics axis presented as a sixth sibling. FairMT-Bench is the "anchor result" in §3.2 *and* "again anchors" §3.6; `ma2026implicit` carries both §3.2 and §3.6. Either make horizon an explicit second axis (component × time) or defend the asymmetry head-on.
- **Tool vs. retrieval boundary is admitted to be porous in the text itself**: §3.1 says "When the 'tool' is a retriever, the bias becomes informational" and then "\cite{wu2024rag} again applies here" in §3.2. If your flagship contribution is the partition, papers cannot float between cells without stated assignment rules. Give explicit decision criteria for tagging a paper to a component.
- **§3.4 Planning is mostly non-agentic CoT work relabeled.** `wu2025reasoning` and `zhou2025veracity` study reasoning bias in QA-style settings; only `parziale2026once` is genuinely about agentic task allocation. This directly contradicts your own inclusion criterion ("Papers exclusively studying static generation tasks without an agentic framing were excluded" — §2.4). Same problem in §3.5: `kantharuban2024stereotype`, `weissburg2024llms`, `lin2024assessing` are single-turn chatbot/recommender studies. Either soften the inclusion criteria to "evidence transferred from adjacent settings, marked as such," or the corpus violates its own methodology. Right now a reviewer can falsify §2.4 with §3.

**Synthesis vs. listing:** §3.7 and §7 are real synthesis — the "formalization tracks decision explicitness, not harm severity" finding (§7.4) is exactly what CSUR wants. But large stretches are citation-chained enumeration:

- §3.2: "Mitigation exists but is partial: \cite{kim2025mitigating} controls the embedder." — a dangling one-clause sentence; `kim2025mitigating` was already cited two sentences earlier in the same paragraph.
- §3.6: "\cite{liu2025truth} and \cite{hong2025measuring} together establish that drift is measurable per-turn" — both already cited in the same paragraph; this sentence adds nothing.
- §4.4 is pure annotated bibliography: "X (FairMedQA) benchmarks… Y provides… Z introduces… W extends…" — five consecutive paragraphs of the pattern CSUR explicitly rejects. The Table should carry the inventory; the prose should carry *analysis* (e.g., why clinical benchmarks all stop at vignettes — incentives? data access? liability?).
- §5.1–5.2 likewise: one-paper-per-sentence summaries, and worse, they re-survey token-level LLM debiasing that §2.4 declared out of scope ("addressed in the parent survey"). §5 should be restructured around the component-mapping claim (Table 3) with token-level families compressed to a paragraph each.

**§3.7's "agentic multiplier" is overclaimed.** The only *evidence* of compounding is intra-multi-agent amplification (`nguyen2025social`, `madigan2025emergent`). The cross-component cascade paragraph ("an attribute inferred by user modeling becomes a retrieved fact… conditions tool selection… delegates along role lines") is a hypothesis dressed as a finding — no cited paper traces a bias across two or more *different* components. Mark it explicitly as conjecture, or it becomes the reviewer's example of the paper believing its own framing.

## 2. Comprehensiveness & currency

Currency is good (61% post-2024, cites into 2026). Comprehensiveness is not CSUR-grade:

- **127 papers is thin.** CSUR taxonomic surveys typically synthesize 200–400. Your own OUTLINE targets 150–200 references but the corpus is 127.
- **Fair ranking / fair IR is nearly absent** despite memory/retrieval being a core component: no Singh & Joachims, Zehlike et al., Ekstrand et al., TREC Fair Ranking. §3.2 rests on a handful of 2024–25 RAG papers while ignoring a decade of directly applicable retrieval-fairness theory.
- **Missing literatures**: fairness impossibility results (Kleinberg et al., Chouldechova) — essential when you propose multiple metric families; LLM-as-judge bias (directly relevant to agent self-evaluation and critic agents in C3); fair RL/bandits beyond two cites (your §3.6 formal grounding is exactly this literature); web/computer-use agent benchmarks (WebArena, τ-bench, AgentBench) — your claim that "no benchmark… presents a fairness evaluation problem at the level of a full agent trajectory" must confront why these can't be demographically instrumented; algorithmic recourse and contestability (invoked in §8.5 without citation); regulation (EU AI Act, NYC LL144 for hiring audits — you discuss hiring audits and governance with zero legal anchoring); social-choice/game-theoretic fairness for multi-agent allocation.
- **§2.4 methodology is under-specified for a "systematic" search**: no counts of hits screened/excluded, no date cutoffs, no screening procedure. The sentence "Candidate papers were verified for existence, accurate metadata, and relevance" is a red flag — verifying that papers *exist* implies the candidate list came from a generative model. Reviewers will infer an LLM-assembled corpus. Reword and describe a PRISMA-style pipeline.

## 3. Structure, flow, balance

- **The draft is formatted for the wrong venue.** Header says "Target venue: ACM SIGKDD Explorations Newsletter." Abstract is one bold-laden paragraph; section budgets are 15–20pp. CSUR needs ~30–40pp, restructured §3 with subsubsections, and full tables (Table 1 doesn't exist in the draft at all; Table 2 is a prose placeholder).
- **§1.4 and §2.4 "Positioning" are near-duplicates** — the same three-neighbor differentiation appears in §1.4, §2.4, §3 opening, §3.7, and §7.4. The "first dedicated survey" claim is made at least five times. Say it once, carefully, with Table 0.
- **§2.2 substantially overlaps §4.4** (both inventory QA benchmarks and state the measurement gap). Merge or differentiate.
- **§6 does not belong in a CSUR survey in its current state** (see §5 below); if retained, it must be complete and compressed.
- §3 components are single wall-of-text "mechanism" paragraphs (§3.2's runs ~300 words with ~10 citations). Break into the promised definition/mechanism/evidence/open-edge sub-headings.
- **Conclusion cites papers never discussed in the body** (`benkirane2024diagnose`, `yang2025prompt`, `bommasani2021opportunities`, `bender2021dangers`) — citation dumping; a reviewer checks this.

## 4. Critical depth & the agenda

§7 and §8 are the strongest sections; §8's problem/why/solution-needs structure is right. Weaknesses:

- **§8.1 and §4.4 advertise "FairMedAgent," your own forthcoming, uncited, unavailable benchmark, as "the empirical instrument the gap demands."** CSUR reviewers treat vaporware self-promotion harshly. Either cite a released artifact or cut to one neutral sentence.
- **Contribution 2 promises an "evaluation framework" with "generalized" CFR/MASD and "articulated" action-level metrics — §4 delivers zero equations.** §4.3 even complains that the three metrics have "no canonical formula" — *you are the survey; supply the formulas.* Define tool-invocation disparity, escalation disparity, and trajectory-level CFR formally. This is the single highest-leverage fix for the originality question.
- No treatment of metric *tensions* in agentic settings (can trajectory-level individual fairness and group parity coexist? what do the impossibility theorems become over trajectories?). A taxonomic survey that maps three metric families without their incompatibilities reads shallow.
- The "NIW framing" leaks: "a matter of national importance" (§1.1), "domains of clear national importance" (§8.5). This is US-immigration-petition language, US-centric, and instrumentally motivated. Remove; CSUR is international.

## 5. Scholarly rigor, honesty, consistency

These are the items that get a paper desk-killed:

1. **FATAL CONTRADICTION:** §6.5 states "The audit has been fully specified but not yet executed… No results are claimed at this stage." The Conclusion states "The original cross-framework audit (§6) **confirmed empirically** what the coverage matrix shows structurally: action-level disparity… is real, measurable, and invisible to today's QA-level scoring." The Abstract states "We **corroborate** it with a small original cross-framework bias audit **exposing** component-level disparity." Claiming results from an unrun experiment is, to a reviewer, scientific dishonesty. Run the audit before submission or rewrite Abstract/§6/Conclusion in protocol-only language everywhere.
2. **Meta-commentary must go:** §6.4 — "We also note its publication role candidly: …the work is positioned to clear arXiv moderation as a *survey with original results*." Strategy talk about gaming moderation inside the manuscript is disqualifying.
3. **Numerical inconsistencies:** §2.4 says "eight branches" then lists **ten**. §7 claims 57 component-tagged papers and "51 of the 57… unspecified," but Figure 2's matrix sums to **68** cells with **52** unspecified. Either papers are multi-tagged (say so and reconcile) or the counts are wrong.
4. **Dwork et al. (2012) is presented under the "Group fairness" heading** (§2.1) — it is the canonical *individual*-fairness paper, and your §2.1 then re-cites it correctly under individual fairness. Any FAccT-adjacent reviewer flags this in the first five minutes. Ground group fairness in demographic parity/equalized-odds lineage instead.
5. **"Exactly one paper" counterfactual claim (§7.1) is fragile:** `mayilvaghanan2026counterfactual` *is* a counterfactual evaluation of a deployed agent; `basu2026names` is counterfactual flip auditing in clinical/judicial decisions. The claim survives only via an undisclosed tagging convention ("named formal framing" within "component-tagged" subset). State the convention precisely or the headline finding looks engineered.
6. Counterfactual-fairness gloss is technically off: "deemed biased only if protected attributes appear in any causal path" — the criterion concerns descendants of the protected attribute under intervention; tighten.
7. Author line "Rohith (first & corresponding)" lacks a surname; draft scaffolding notes (header block) must be stripped.

## 6. Required revisions

### Must-fix (acceptance-blocking)
1. **Resolve the §6 contradiction**: execute the audit and report results, or rewrite Abstract, §6, and Conclusion as protocol-only — no "corroborate/confirmed/exposing" language anywhere. (Abstract, §6, §9)
2. **Delete the arXiv-moderation meta-commentary** and all pipeline scaffolding (header notes, "NIW framing," venue line). (§6.4, header, §1.1, §8.5)
3. **Reconcile all corpus numbers**: 127 papers, 8-vs-10 branches, 57-vs-68 tagged entries, 51-vs-52 unspecified; publish the tagging rules. (§2.4, §7, Fig 2)
4. **Fix the Dwork misattribution** and the counterfactual-fairness definition. (§2.1)
5. **Formally define the action-level metrics and the CFR/MASD trajectory generalization with equations** — deliver Contribution 2. (§4.2–4.3)
6. **Repair the taxonomy's category structure**: justify long-horizon drift as component-vs-axis; give explicit component-assignment rules; resolve the tool/retrieval overlap. (§3)
7. **Align inclusion criteria with actual corpus**: either admit transferred non-agentic evidence explicitly per component or restrict claims. (§2.4 vs §3.4/§3.5)
8. **Rewrite §4.4, §5.1–5.2 from annotated-bibliography prose into comparative synthesis**; move inventories into the (currently non-existent) Tables 1–2, which must be built. (§4.4, §5)
9. **Reposition for CSUR**: ~30–40pp, expanded corpus toward ≥180–200 references, CSUR-style structure; the draft is currently a KDD Explorations paper. (global)

### Should-fix
10. Add fair-ranking/IR foundations to §3.2; fair-RL/sequential foundations expanded in §3.6; impossibility theorems and their trajectory-level analogues in §4.1. (§3.2, §3.6, §4.1)
11. Confront WebArena/τ-bench/AgentBench-class agent benchmarks when claiming no trajectory-level fairness benchmark exists. (§4.4)
12. Downgrade §3.7's cross-component cascade to explicit conjecture; keep the multi-agent amplification as the only evidenced case. (§3.7)
13. Cut FairMedAgent promotion to one neutral sentence or cite a released artifact. (§4.4, §8.1, §8 synthesis)
14. Deduplicate positioning (keep §1.4 + Table 0; cut repeats in §2.4, §3, §3.7, §7.4); state "first dedicated taxonomy" once. (§1–§3, §7)
15. Merge or differentiate §2.2 and §4.4; remove conclusion-only citations or discuss them in the body. (§2.2, §9)
16. Describe the systematic search properly (queries, screening counts, dates); remove "verified for existence." (§2.4)
17. Add legal/governance anchoring (EU AI Act, NYC LL144, model audit regimes) to §8.5. (§8.5)

### Nice-to-have
18. A worked end-to-end example (one hiring scenario traced through all six components) early in §3 to make the taxonomy concrete. (§3 intro)
19. Maturity ratings (per FIGURES.md Table 1 spec) surfaced in the §3 prose so each component's evidence strength is graded. (§3, Table 1)
20. Discussion of fairness–utility and fairness–privacy tensions in user modeling (you cite `vijjini2024exploring` and `staab2023memorization` but never synthesize the tension). (§3.5)
21. Colorblind-safe heatmap and a "partial" category criterion for Table 2's Agentic? column (mFARM is "partial" with no stated rule). (Fig 2, Table 2)

**Bottom line:** the component-×-dimension coverage matrix and the measurement-gap thesis are a credible CSUR backbone. But the paper currently claims results it does not have, contradicts its own scope and counts, under-delivers its promised formal framework, and lists where it should synthesize. Fix items 1–9 and this becomes a serious candidate; submit as-is and it is rejected on item 1 alone.

Files reviewed: `C:\Users\rohit\Documents\Research Papers\research-command-center\06_paper_pipeline\AGENT_FAIRNESS_SURVEY\AGENT_FAIRNESS_SURVEY.md`, `...\OUTLINE.md`, `...\FIGURES.md`.

(a) VERDICT — BORDERLINE: original organizing principle, but not yet a FRAMEWORK by CSUR standards

What genuinely passes:
- The component-locus organizing axis (§3.1–3.6) is original and defended against four named near-neighbors (§1.4, §3 preamble): Chu/Zhang organize by metric, Mohammadi by eval dimension, Vatsal by clinical dimension, Mayilvaghanan by single-domain method. No prior work uses "where in the agent pipeline bias enters" as the primary axis. (Lines 111, 45 of the draft.)
- Each §3 cell has structure beyond bibliography: Definition / Mechanism-of-harm / Evidence / Open-edge (e.g., §3.1 lines 117–121). That is synthesis, not listing.
- §7's coverage matrix DERIVES non-obvious findings ("formalization tracks decision explicitness, not harm severity"; "the counterfactual column is essentially empty"; 51/57 papers unspecified-dimension) — these are analytical products of the taxonomy, evidence of explanatory power.
- §6 pre-registers hypotheses (H1/H2) generated by the taxonomy — a predictive use.

(b) STRENGTHENED FRAMEWORK SPEC — "the Bias Conduction Framework (BCF)" (ready to write into §3)

Two-axis model: Axis 1 = ENTRY LOCUS (the existing six components, unchanged). Axis 2 = CONDUCTION OPERATOR — how a disparity introduced at one locus is transformed as it crosses to the next pipeline stage. Every corpus paper becomes a (locus, operator) coordinate; every metric and mitigation is keyed to a coordinate.

Formal core (new §3.0, ~2 pages):

D1 (Agent loop). An agent is a tuple ⟨π, T, M, R⟩ over steps t = 1..T: state s_t = (h_t, m_t) (history, memory); input x contains protected attribute A; action a_t = π(s_t, x) drawn from {tool calls, retrievals, delegations, plan steps, escalations, final decision y}. A trajectory is τ(x) = (a_1, …, a_T, y).

D2 (Component counterfactual disparity). For component c, Δ_c(x) = E[ d( τ(x)↾c , τ(x_{A←a′})↾c ) ] — the expected divergence of the component's action variable under a demographic swap, holding upstream realizations fixed (trace replay). This is the formal object that CFR/MASD (§4.2), tool-invocation disparity, escalation disparity, and plan-allocation disparity (§4.3) all instantiate — supply the canonical formulas §4.3 currently says don't exist.

D3 (Conduction operators). Each pipeline edge c→c′ applies an operator φ with four modes:
- ATTENUATE (φ<1): downstream processing damps the disparity (e.g., debate with identity anonymization — choi2025identity).
- PRESERVE (φ≈1): disparity carried unchanged (memory write of an inferred attribute — staab2023memorization → fan2024fairmt).
- AMPLIFY (φ>1): disparity grows across the edge (multi-agent interaction — nguyen2025social; sycophantic drift — liu2025truth).
- GENERATE (φ acts on zero input): disparity emerges where none entered (emergent multi-agent bias — madigan2025emergent; reasoning-introduced bias — wu2025reasoning).

Conduction equation (first-order): Δ_τ ≈ Σ_c Δ_c · Π_{e ∈ path(c→y)} φ_e, with feedback edges (memory write-back, self-training) giving the recurrence Δ^(t+1) = φ_fb · Δ^(t) + Δ_entry — drift is geometric when φ_fb > 1 (this FORMALIZES §3.6 and wyllie2024fairness/xu2024adapting instead of citing them).

Five propositions the framework yields (these are the "derived insights" CSUR wants):
- P1 (Locality). Every Δ_c is separately measurable by component-level counterfactual intervention with trace replay; bias attribution is well-posed per locus. (Grounded by xu2026ducx's stage decomposition.)
- P2 (Masking). Endpoint token-level parity places no bound on Δ_τ at the action level, because attenuation at the actuation surface can co-exist with amplification upstream. (Explains li2025actions; this IS the measurement gap, now as a theorem-shaped claim.)
- P3 (Super-additivity / the agentic multiplier). Δ_τ exceeds Σ_c Δ_c iff the path contains at least one AMPLIFY or GENERATE edge — i.e., a state-writing or inter-agent edge. Predicts: architectures with memory write-back or delegation compound; stateless tool pipelines do not. (This is exactly H2 of §6, now derived rather than asserted.)
- P4 (Mitigation matching). An intervention reduces Δ_τ only if it acts at or upstream of the entry locus AND on the active operator. Predicts the §5 finding (token-level debiasing fails for tool-routing bias) instead of merely observing it; reorganizes Table 3 as (locus × operator) coverage.
- P5 (Measurement adequacy). A benchmark certifies locus c only if it observes c's action variable under counterfactual swap. Corollary: every "Agentic? = no" row of Table 2 certifies nothing about Δ_c for c ≠ final answer — the §4.4 punchline, derived.

Worked example (new §3.8, ~1 page): walk a hiring-screener agent (the §6 C0→C3 ladder) through BCF prospectively. C0: single locus, no edges → Δ_τ = Δ_decision (QA audits suffice). C1: adds tool-selection locus + PRESERVE edge → P2 predicts masking (= H1). C2: adds memory/plan loci + feedback edge (φ_fb) → recurrence predicts intra-trajectory drift. C3: adds delegation locus + GENERATE/AMPLIFY edges → P3 predicts super-additivity (= H2), with choi2025identity's anonymization as the P4-predicted counter-lever. Close with the analysis recipe (4 steps: enumerate loci → classify edges by operator → instrument Δ_c per P1/P5 → match mitigations per P4) any auditor can apply to a new system.

### 9. "HOW TO USE THIS SURVEY" GUIDE FOR PRACTITIONERS AND REGULATORS (Medium — CSUR impact value)

**Why CSUR expects it.** CSUR's stated mission includes serving practitioners and policymakers, not only researchers. For a survey on fairness in high-stakes agent deployments, a brief navigation guide substantially increases the paper's impact and is specifically mentioned in CSUR editorial guidelines as a best practice for applied topics.

**What the draft has.** None. The paper is organized purely for a research reader.

**What is missing.**
- A short (quarter-page) box or sidebar: "How to Use This Survey." Four reader paths: (a) AI practitioner deploying an agent in a consequential domain → start at §3 for your component, go to §5 for mitigations, check Table 3; (b) auditor or regulator evaluating an agent for fairness compliance → start at §4.2–4.3 for metrics, §8.5 for governance gaps; (c) researcher entering the field → read §2–3 for foundations, §7 for the coverage map showing where to contribute; (d) benchmark builder → §4.4 for the measurement gap, §8.1 for what FairMedAgent needs to cover.
- In the absence of a box, at minimum a paragraph at the end of §1 (after contributions and positioning) that directs different reader types to the relevant sections.

**Where to add.** End of §1 or a callout box in §2.

**Effort.** Very low — purely additive prose.

---

### 10. FUTURE-WORK CONCRETENESS AND ACTIONABILITY (Medium — distinguishes CSUR from workshop papers)

**Why CSUR expects it.** Section 8 exists and is substantive, but CSUR reviewers distinguish between open problems that are "directions" (vague) and open problems that are "research specifications" (actionable). The current §8 mostly describes *what* is missing; it does not consistently specify *what a solution would look like* in operational terms — the protocol, dataset structure, metric formula, or system design that would close the gap.

**What the draft has.** §8.1 is the strongest: it specifies FairMedAgent at a reasonable level of detail. §8.2–8.6 are thinner — they state the problem and say "what a solution needs" but at a level of generality that does not give a researcher a concrete starting point.

**What is missing.**
- For each §8.x open problem, one concrete "first step" — a specific research question, a proposed dataset structure, a proposed metric formula, or a named system design. Example for §8.2: "A long-horizon fairness benchmark would need at minimum M simulated multi-turn sessions per demographic group, covering at least K decision points per session, and would report equal long-term benefit rate [Xu et al.] as the primary metric alongside CFR computed over the full trajectory." Example for §8.3: "A multi-agent amplification metric would quantify the ratio of system-level disparity to the maximum per-agent disparity, with a value > 1 indicating emergence."
- For §8.5 (governance), a concrete mapping to existing regulatory frameworks: EU AI Act Article 10/13 data and transparency requirements, US EEOC guidance on automated hiring tools, NIST AI RMF. This grounds the governance gap in existing instruments that practitioners and regulators actually use, and substantially strengthens the national-importance argument.

**Where to add.** One to three sentences added to each §8.x "What a solution needs" block.

**Effort.** Moderate — requires domain knowledge of regulatory frameworks and careful operationalization, but no new literature review.

### 11. PRIOR SURVEY COMPARISON ON CORPUS COVERAGE (Lower priority but noticed)

**Why CSUR expects it.** CSUR reviewers sometimes ask: does this survey cover papers the prior surveys missed? A brief note (or column in Table 0) showing that the 127-paper corpus includes a set of papers not in Gallegos et al. 2024 or Chu et al. 2024 would demonstrate that the survey has not simply re-organized the same literature but has genuinely found new material by targeting the agentic subfield.

**What the draft has.** None of this comparison.

**What is missing.** In Table 0, add a column "Unique papers not in prior surveys (approx.)" or a footnote. Even a rough count is useful. Alternatively, a sentence in §2.4: "Of the 127 papers in our corpus, approximately N appear in neither Gallegos et al. nor Chu et al., reflecting the post-2023 agentic literature that those surveys predate."

**Where to add.** §2.4 or Table 0 footnote.

**Effort.** Very low.

## Critical Observation on Target Venue

The OUTLINE.md and PROTOCOL.md both target **ACM SIGKDD Explorations Newsletter**, not CSUR. SIGKDD Explorations has a substantially lower bar: it accepts 15–20pp survey/overview pieces, does not require PRISMA-style methodology, and is oriented toward the KDD community rather than the computing-as-a-whole CSUR audience. If the actual target is CSUR, the paper requires approximately 10,000–15,000 additional words (CSUR surveys typically run 30–60 pages in double-column), a far more comprehensive corpus (CSUR expects 200–400+ papers for a mature topic), and the full set of gaps identified above as mandatory rather than recommended. If the target remains SIGKDD Explorations, gaps 1–5 are still strongly recommended but the scope is achievable at the current draft length. The decision of which venue to target should be made explicitly and the paper tuned accordingly — the current draft is appropriately sized for SIGKDD Explorations but would need substantial expansion for CSUR.
